concept_name: Upstream vs Downstream — River Flow Adds or Subtracts
source_book: DC Pandey 6.11 — river-boat upstream/downstream; split from river_boat_problems

real_world_anchor.primary: A motorboat on the Ganga near Varanasi. The river current flows downstream at 4 km/h. The boat's engine can push it through still water at 10 km/h. When the pilot points the boat downstream (toward the sea), the river helps — ground speed is 10 + 4 = 14 km/h. When he turns around and heads upstream (against the current), the river fights back — ground speed is only 10 - 4 = 6 km/h. Same boat, same engine, same river — the only change is direction.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A kayaker on the Brahmaputra during the monsoon. Her paddling speed in still water is 8 km/h; the river is flowing at 6 km/h. Going downstream she zips along at 14 km/h. Going upstream she barely creeps forward at 2 km/h — any slower and the current would carry her backward.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A swimmer training against a treadmill pool current. The water flows at 1 m/s toward her; she swims at 1.2 m/s in still water. Her net ground speed is only 0.2 m/s forward — she stays almost in place. Upstream/downstream is everywhere water meets motion.

formulas.downstream: Going downstream: v_ground = v_boat + v_river  (current helps)
formulas.upstream: Going upstream:   v_ground = v_boat - v_river  (current resists)
formulas.general_relative: Both formulas come from V_boat_ground = V_boat_water + V_water_ground. Signs of each velocity determine the result.
formulas.stall_condition: If v_river >= v_boat, the boat cannot make upstream progress — it drifts backward.

constraints: Requires current along the same axis as boat motion.
Boat speed is always measured WITH RESPECT TO WATER (engine power), never ground.
For any 1D river problem, write down v_boat and v_river with signs, then add.

scene_composition.text:
Ganga downstream near Varanasi:

  Boat speed (still water):   v_boat   = 10 km/h
  River current:               v_river  =  4 km/h
  Direction: boat goes WITH the flow

  Ground speed  =  v_boat + v_river
               =  10 + 4
               =  14 km/h

  Going downstream feels effortless -- the river
  carries you along.

  Same engine power, bigger ground speed.  Free kilometres.
Motorboat on the Ganga, Varanasi to Patna direction

tts_sentences.text_en: A motorboat heading downstream on the Ganga. The pilot's still-water speed is 10 kilometres per hour; the current flows at 4.
Because the current pushes in the SAME direction as the boat, their contributions add.
Ground speed is 10 plus 4 — 14 kilometres per hour.
Going downstream feels effortless. The engine delivers 10, and the river gifts another 4. Same throttle, bigger result.

scene_composition.text:
Same Ganga, same boat, REVERSED:

  Boat speed:    v_boat   = 10 km/h  (upstream)
  River current: v_river  = +4 km/h   (still downstream)

  Now engine and current POINT OPPOSITE WAYS.

  Ground speed  =  v_boat - v_river
               =  10 - 4
               =  6 km/h upstream

  The engine still pushes the boat at 10 through water,
  but the water itself is sliding backward at 4.

  Effective ground gain is only 6 km/h.
Same boat, now pointed upstream toward Haridwar

tts_sentences.text_en: Now reverse it — the pilot turns the boat upstream, toward Haridwar.
The engine still drives the boat at 10 through water; but the water itself is sliding downstream at 4.
The two velocities now oppose each other. Ground speed is 10 minus 4 — only 6 kilometres per hour.
Same boat, same engine, same river, same 10-km-per-hour muscle — but the ground progress is less than half of the downstream case.

STATE_3.title: Stall Condition — When the Boat Cannot Make Progress

scene_composition.text:
What if the current is STRONGER than the boat?

  v_boat   = 4 km/h  (upstream)
  v_river  = 6 km/h  (downstream)

  Ground speed  =  4 - 6  =  -2 km/h

  Negative ground speed means the boat DRIFTS BACKWARD
  even with the engine on.  The river wins.

  Three cases:
    v_boat >  v_river   -> progress possible
    v_boat =  v_river   -> stays in place (stalemate)
    v_boat <  v_river   -> drifts backward (lost)

  Real world: small dinghies during Brahmaputra monsoon
  flood simply cannot go upstream.
Stall Condition — When the Boat Cannot Make Progress
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: One edge case worth remembering — what if the river is stronger than the boat?
Boat at 4 kilometres per hour, current at 6. Attempting to go upstream gives 4 minus 6 — negative 2.
Negative ground speed means the boat drifts backward even with the engine running at full throttle.
Three regimes: faster boat makes progress; equal speeds stall the boat in place; slower boat loses ground. During a Brahmaputra monsoon flood, small dinghies literally cannot go upstream.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student treats v_boat as if it were the ground speed, ignoring that the engine's speed is measured relative to the water.
confusion_patterns: why add current to boat speed

STATE_1.title: The Confusion: Whose Frame Does v_boat Live In?

scene_composition.text:
The confusing question:
  "If the boat does 10 km/h, isn't that
   already the ground speed?"

  No. v_boat is measured relative to the WATER,
  because the engine pushes against water, not ground.

  The water itself is moving, so ground speed differs.

  Mistake: treating v_boat as if measured from the shore.
  Correct: v_boat is always engine-vs-water, never boat-vs-ground.
The Confusion: Whose Frame Does v_boat Live In?
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: A student asks — if the boat does 10, isn't that already the ground speed?
No. v boat is measured against the WATER, because that is what the propeller pushes against.
And the water itself is moving. So the ground speed is something else entirely — depending on which direction the boat points.
Mistake: treating v boat as shore-relative. Correct: v boat is engine-against-water, nothing else.

scene_composition.text:
Write it with subscripts:

  V_boat/water  = 10 km/h   (engine spec, always this)
  V_water/ground = 4 km/h    (river current)

  Combine using the chain rule of relative velocity:

     V_boat/ground = V_boat/water + V_water/ground

  Downstream (aligned):  V_boat/ground = 10 + 4 = 14 km/h
  Upstream (opposed):    V_boat/ground = 10 - 4 = 6 km/h

  The subscripts track WHO is measuring WHOM.
  No more frame confusion.
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: The cure is to write every velocity with its reference frame in subscript.
V boat-slash-water is the engine speed — always 10.
V water-slash-ground is the river speed — 4.
Combine them: V boat-slash-ground equals V boat-slash-water plus V water-slash-ground. Downstream this is 14, upstream it is 6.
Subscripts stop frame confusion dead. Always write them on river problems.

STATE_3.title: Always Think: Engine Speed vs Ground Speed

scene_composition.text:
Golden rule:

  Engine speed  = v_boat = ALWAYS relative to water
  Ground speed  = what an observer on the shore sees
                 = v_boat + v_river (aligned)
                 = v_boat - v_river (opposed)

  One of your exam instincts should be:
    'When I read v_boat = 10 km/h,
     I translate: engine pushes the hull through water at 10 km/h.
     The WATER itself has a separate motion.'

  That single habit kills 90% of river-boat mistakes.
Always Think: Engine Speed vs Ground Speed
(scene under construction — primitives backfilled to satisfy minimum)

tts_sentences.text_en: Golden rule — engine speed is always against water, and ground speed always includes the river.
Whenever you see v boat equals 10, translate automatically: the hull moves through water at 10, and the water itself is moving separately.
That one-second translation kills ninety percent of river-boat mistakes before they happen.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Belief: Think Boat Speed Is Ground (auto-generated from branch_id — needs author rewrite).
